slides.title: Where Can a Variable Be Seen?
slides.body: Every variable has a **scope** — the region of code where it exists and can be used. Think of variables like notebooks in different rooms: a notebook in the bedroom can't be read from the kitchen.
slides.tip: Scope is why a variable can seem to 'disappear' inside a function.

slides.title: Local Variables Live Inside Functions
slides.body: A variable created **inside** a function is **local** — it exists only while the function runs, then vanishes. Code outside the function can't see it at all.
slides.code:
def make_greeting():
    message = "Hello!"
    print(message)

make_greeting()
print(message)  # NameError!
slides.output:
Hello!
NameError: name 'message' is not defined
slides.tip: Local variables are born when the function runs and gone when it ends.

slides.title: Global Variables Are Visible Everywhere
slides.body: A variable created **outside** any function lives in **global** scope. Functions in the same file can **read** it freely — but modifying it is a different story.
slides.code:
country = "Canada"

def show_country():
    print(country)  # reading a global is fine

show_country()

slides.title: Python Searches by the LEGB Rule
slides.body: When Python sees a variable name it searches: **L**ocal → **E**nclosing → **G**lobal → **B**uilt-in. It stops at the first match — or raises a `NameError` if nothing is found.
slides.code:
x = "global"

def outer():
    x = "enclosing"
    def inner():
        print(x)  # finds enclosing first
    inner()

outer()
slides.tip: Built-ins like `print` and `len` are at level B — always available.

slides.title: Assigning Inside a Function Makes It Local
slides.body: The moment Python sees `score = ...` inside a function, it treats **every** use of `score` in that function as local — even lines *before* the assignment. Use `global score` to modify the top-level variable instead.
slides.code:
score = 0

def add_point():
    global score
    score = score + 1

add_point()
add_point()
print(score)
slides.tip: Prefer passing values as arguments and returning results — it keeps code clean.

slides.title: Keep Data Local, Pass It Explicitly
slides.body: You've got scope! Local variables are safe and self-contained; globals can cause sneaky bugs. **Pass data in as arguments, return it back out** — and your functions will be easy to read and test.
slides.tip: Default to local scope; reach for `global` only when you truly need to.
